- I drafted a concise BLUEPRINT in `/.cursor/rules/workflow-state.mdc` proposing a minimal UART ISR that only enqueues bytes into a ring buffer and moves all parsing to the main loop. It’s marked NEEDS_PLAN_APPROVAL.

### 1) Is re-adding an ISR with a ring buffer sensible?
- Yes. A minimal RX ISR that only enqueues bytes prevents drops when the main loop is busy (audio, logging), while keeping ISR time tiny. This improves robustness without materially complicating the design. Responsiveness will be at least as good as polling, and reliability under bursty UART input will be better. Keep ISR logic trivial; do all parsing and protocol handling in the main loop.

### 2) Plan to implement and then restore bidirectional tests
- Enable RX interrupt for `USART1` and add a tiny ring buffer enqueue in the ISR; do not parse in the ISR.
- Drain the ring buffer in the main loop via a small framing state machine, then call `ProcessUARTMessage(...)`.
- Keep TX as-is (build packet in main loop, blocking transmit), with optional low-rate heartbeat.
- Restore the original main loop intent: start audio, run `PollAndParseUART()` each iteration, and test bidirectional messages (PARAM updates, SAMPLE_CTRL, PREVIEW_REQ, METER_PUSH, WAVE_CHUNK).

Implementation highlights (summarized from the BLUEPRINT):
- In `firmware/daisy/src/main.cpp`:
  - Keep/expand `rx_ring`, `rx_head/tail`, `rx_total`, `g_uart_rx_errors` as `volatile`.
  - Add `rx_enqueue_from_isr(uint8_t ch)` and `rx_dequeue_byte(...)`.
  - Enable RXNE IRQ after `HAL_UART_Init(&huart1)`.
  - Provide a minimal `USART1_IRQHandler` that reads `RDR`, enqueues, and clears error flags; no parsing, no logging.
  - Add `PollAndParseUART()` (SYNC 0xAA, length, type, payload, CRC-16) and call it in the main loop.
  - Re-enable audio with `hw.StartAudio(AudioCallback);` and remove the temporary tight loop prints.

Testing (quick stages):
- With audio stopped: flood RX from ESP32, confirm no hang/overrun, `rx_total` rises.
- With audio running: repeat flood; verify no underruns/hangs.
- End-to-end with ESP32: PARAM, SAMPLE_CTRL, PREVIEW_REQ in; METER_PUSH/WAVE_CHUNK out; CRC valid.

Would you like me to proceed implementing exactly this BLUEPRINT now?

- If you prefer avoiding a custom IRQ handler, I can instead use `HAL_UART_Receive_IT(..., 1)` and an ultra-minimal `HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback` that enqueues one byte and re-arms; it’s even simpler but previously the HAL path seemed implicated in hangs. Your call.

-// Minimal RX ring buffer for ISR-safe byte capture
-static volatile uint8_t  rx_ring[256];
-static volatile uint16_t rx_head = 0;

-static volatile uint32_t rx_total = 0;
+// Minimal RX ring buffer for ISR-safe byte capture
+static constexpr uint16_t RX_RING_SIZE = 512;
+static constexpr uint16_t RX_RING_MASK = RX_RING_SIZE - 1; // requires power-of-two size
+static volatile uint8_t  rx_ring[RX_RING_SIZE];
+static volatile uint16_t rx_head = 0;
+static volatile uint16_t rx_tail = 0;
+static volatile uint32_t rx_total = 0;
@@
 // Forward declarations
 void ProcessUARTMessage(const uint8_t* buffer, size_t length);
 void InitUART(void);
 void TestUARTLoopback(void);
+static void PollAndParseUART(void);
+static inline void rx_enqueue_from_isr(uint8_t ch);
+static inline int rx_dequeue_byte(uint8_t &out_byte);
@@
 static volatile uint32_t g_uart_rx_count = 0;
 static volatile uint32_t g_uart_rx_errors = 0;
 static volatile uint32_t g_uart_tx_count = 0;
 static volatile uint32_t g_uart_tx_errors = 0;
 static volatile uint32_t g_last_uart_activity = 0;
@@
 }
 
+// === RX ring buffer helpers ===
+static inline void rx_enqueue_from_isr(uint8_t ch)
+{
+    uint16_t next_head = (uint16_t)((rx_head + 1) & RX_RING_MASK);
+    if(next_head == rx_tail)
+    {
+        // Overflow: drop oldest byte to make room
+        rx_tail = (uint16_t)((rx_tail + 1) & RX_RING_MASK);
+        g_uart_rx_errors++;
+    }
+    rx_ring[rx_head] = ch;
+    rx_head = next_head;
+    rx_total++;
+}
+
+static inline int rx_dequeue_byte(uint8_t &out_byte)
+{
+    if(rx_tail == rx_head)
+    {
+        return 0;
+    }
+    out_byte = rx_ring[rx_tail];
+    rx_tail = (uint16_t)((rx_tail + 1) & RX_RING_MASK);
+    return 1;
+}
+
+// === USART1 IRQ Handler: minimal, enqueue-only ===
+extern "C" void USART1_IRQHandler(void)
+{
+    // Drain all available bytes
+#if defined(UART_FLAG_RXNE_RXFNE)
+    while(__HAL_UART_GET_FLAG(&huart1, UART_FLAG_RXNE_RXFNE) != RESET)
+#else
+    while(__HAL_UART_GET_FLAG(&huart1, UART_FLAG_RXNE) != RESET)
+#endif
+    {
+        uint8_t ch = static_cast<uint8_t>(huart1.Instance->RDR & 0xFFu);
+        rx_enqueue_from_isr(ch);
+        g_uart_rx_count++;
+        g_last_uart_activity = System::GetNow();
+    }
+
+    // Clear common error conditions if present (by reading RDR above, ORE is cleared)
+    // Optionally acknowledge other error flags to avoid spurious IRQs
+#ifdef UART_CLEAR_OREF
+    if(__HAL_UART_GET_FLAG(&huart1, UART_FLAG_ORE) != RESET)
+    {
+        __HAL_UART_CLEAR_FLAG(&huart1, UART_CLEAR_OREF);
+    }
+#endif
+}
+

<<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy/src/main.cpp
@@
 void AudioCallback(AudioHandle::InputBuffer  in,
@@
     Timebase::Tick1kHz([]{
@@
         // Meter push every 20 ms
         static uint32_t meter_ctr = 0;
         if(++meter_ctr >= 20) {
             meter_ctr = 0;
             float rms=0, peak=0;
             Sampler::BlockMeters(g_last_in_block, g_last_block_size, rms, peak);
             MeterPushMessage m{rms, peak};
             // Queue the message instead of trying to send directly
             QueueMessage(MSG_METER_PUSH, &m, sizeof(m));
         }
@@
     });
 }
+
+// === UART frame parser (runs in main loop) ===
+static void PollAndParseUART(void)
+{
+    enum ParserState { FIND_SYNC, READ_LEN_HI, READ_LEN_LO, READ_TYPE, READ_PAYLOAD, READ_CRC_HI, READ_CRC_LO };
+    static ParserState state = FIND_SYNC;
+    static uint8_t frame[512];
+    static uint16_t expected_len = 0;
+    static uint16_t idx = 0;
+    static uint8_t msg_type = 0;
+    uint8_t byte = 0;
+
+    while(rx_dequeue_byte(byte))
+    {
+        switch(state)
+        {
+            case FIND_SYNC:
+                if(byte == 0xAA) { state = READ_LEN_HI; idx = 0; }
+                break;
+            case READ_LEN_HI:
+                expected_len = static_cast<uint16_t>(byte) << 8;
+                state = READ_LEN_LO;
+                break;
+            case READ_LEN_LO:
+                expected_len |= byte;
+                if(expected_len > sizeof(frame)) { state = FIND_SYNC; }
+                else { state = READ_TYPE; }
+                break;
+            case READ_TYPE:
+                msg_type = byte; // currently unused here; `ProcessUARTMessage` will parse full frame
+                state = (expected_len == 0) ? READ_CRC_HI : READ_PAYLOAD;
+                idx = 0;
+                break;
+            case READ_PAYLOAD:
+                frame[idx++] = byte;
+                if(idx >= expected_len)
+                {
+                    state = READ_CRC_HI;
+                }
+                break;
+            case READ_CRC_HI:
+                // Defer CRC verification to ProtocolHandler::ValidatePacket
+                // Accumulate into a temp buffer: [SYNC][LEN hi][LEN lo][TYPE][PAYLOAD][CRC hi]
+                {
+                    static uint8_t packet[520];
+                    size_t total = 0;
+                    packet[total++] = 0xAA;
+                    packet[total++] = static_cast<uint8_t>((expected_len >> 8) & 0xFF);
+                    packet[total++] = static_cast<uint8_t>(expected_len & 0xFF);
+                    packet[total++] = msg_type;
+                    memcpy(&packet[total], frame, expected_len);
+                    total += expected_len;
+                    packet[total++] = byte; // CRC hi
+
+                    // Need one more byte (CRC lo) → move to next state and stash total in idx (abuse idx as temp)
+                    idx = static_cast<uint16_t>(total);
+                    state = READ_CRC_LO;
+                }
+                break;
+            case READ_CRC_LO:
+                {
+                    static uint8_t packet[520];
+                    size_t total = idx; // from previous state
+                    packet[total++] = byte; // CRC lo
+
+                    // Process complete packet
+                    ProcessUARTMessage(packet, total);
+                }
+                // Reset for next frame
+                state = FIND_SYNC;
+                idx = 0;
+                expected_len = 0;
+                break;
+        }
+    }
+}
*** End Patch
PATCH
```

I'll remove the ISR I've added and any interrupt enabling code to avoid conflicts.

 static inline int rx_dequeue_byte(uint8_t &out_byte)
 {
     if(rx_tail == rx_head)
     {
         return 0;
     }
     out_byte = rx_ring[rx_tail];
     rx_tail = (uint16_t)((rx_tail + 1) & RX_RING_MASK);
     return 1;
 }
 
-// No custom IRQ handler here; libDaisy provides UART IRQ vectors. We use polling to fill the ring.
+// Poll hardware flags to move bytes from RDR into ring (non-blocking)
+static inline void PollUartHardwareToRing(void)
+{
+#if defined(UART_FLAG_RXNE_RXFNE)
+    while(__HAL_UART_GET_FLAG(&huart1, UART_FLAG_RXNE_RXFNE) != RESET)
+#else
+    while(__HAL_UART_GET_FLAG(&huart1, UART_FLAG_RXNE) != RESET)
+#endif
+    {
+        uint8_t ch = static_cast<uint8_t>(huart1.Instance->RDR & 0xFFu);
+        rx_enqueue_from_isr(ch);
+        g_uart_rx_count++;
+        g_last_uart_activity = System::GetNow();
+    }
+}
*** End Patch
```

+// === Transmit any queued messages without waiting for requests ===
+static void ServiceUartTxQueue(void)
+{
+    // Send at most a few packets per loop to avoid starving other work
+    for(int i = 0; i < 2; ++i)
+    {
+        QueuedMessage msg;
+        if(!GetNextQueuedMessage(msg))
+            break;
+        PrepareResponsePacket(msg);
+    }
+}

<details>
            <summary>Searched web `site:github.com libDaisy UART_IRQHandler USART1_IRQHandler uart.cpp Daisy Seed` • **1** results</summary>

|-------|-----|-------|
| Web Search Results |  | In the libDaisy framework, the `USART1_IRQHandler` function serves as the interrupt handler for the USART1 peripheral. This function is automatically invoked by the system when USART1 generates an interrupt, such as when data is received or when a transmission is complete.<br/><br/>Within libDaisy, the `USART1_IRQHandler` is typically defined to call the HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) function `HAL_UART_IRQHandler(&huart1);`. This HAL function processes the interrupt by determining its cause—be it data reception, transmission completion, or an error—and subsequently calls the appropriate callback functions. For instance, upon completing a data reception, the HAL may invoke `HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback`, where user-defined code can handle the received data. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/65590439/i-am-trying-to-use-uart-communication-in-interrupt-mode-where-should-i-put-my-f?utm_source=openai))<br/><br/>It's important to note that if you define your own `USART1_IRQHandler` in your application code, it will override the default handler provided by libDaisy. This can be useful for implementing custom interrupt handling logic but requires careful management to avoid conflicts. For example, multiple definitions of `USART1_IRQHandler` can lead to compilation errors. ([community.st.com](https://community.st.com/t5/stm32-mcus-motor-control/multiple-definition-of-usart1-irqhandler-after-enabling-global/td-p/107160?utm_source=openai))<br/><br/>In summary, the `USART1_IRQHandler` in libDaisy acts as the entry point for handling USART1 interrupts, delegating the specific processing to HAL functions and user-defined callbacks. |

</details>

  - Main loop now:
    - Polls RX into ring, parses frames, applies control in `ProcessUARTMessage(...)`.
    - Proactively sends queued outbounds via `ServiceUartTxQueue()` for higher TX throughput.
    - Logs counters every ~1000 iterations.

What libDaisy’s USART1 IRQ handler does:
- libDaisy defines UART IRQ vectors centrally (see `libs/libDaisy/src/per/uart.cpp`):
  - `extern "C" void USART1_IRQHandler() { UART_IRQHandler(&uart_handles[0]); }`
  - `UART_IRQHandler(...)` calls `HAL_UART_IRQHandler(&huart_)` which services RX/TX/error and dispatches HAL callbacks.
  - If in “listen” (circular DMA) mode and IDLE flag is set, it calls an internal callback to drain the DMA ring, then clears IDLE.
  - Otherwise it routes to DMA completion logic or user circular-RX callback depending on mode.
- Implication: if you also define your own `USART1_IRQHandler` you will conflict/override libDaisy’s. Our current approach avoids this by not adding our own handler and using non-blocking polling to move bytes into our ring.

Next actions I can run if you want:
- On ESP32: add a short test task to spam control changes and preview requests for 10–30 seconds to stress Daisy; log stats.
- On Daisy: temporarily bump the loop status log to include `rx_total` and queue depth; optionally add a 1 Hz heartbeat `MSG_SYNC`.

- Implementation impact: None further right now. Both sides already have the plumbing to run this test. Just trigger the ESP32 send APIs in a loop and observe logs on both ends.

- If you prefer true interrupt-driven RX on Daisy later, we can switch to HAL `HAL_UART_Receive_IT(..., 1)` with a one-byte rearm in `HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback` and ensure libDaisy is not owning USART1 for that mode.

Model: GPT-5, 175B, multimodal, rev 2025-08-15

### Short answer
- No, not directly. libDaisy already defines the HAL UART callbacks and the `USART1_IRQHandler`. If you add your own `HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback`/friends, you’ll hit multiple-definition conflicts. They’re not weak in libDaisy, so you can’t safely override them from our app.

### Safe options
- Use libDaisy’s extension point: its `UartHandler` supports a circular DMA “listen” mode with a user callback. We can initialize `UartHandler` on USART1 and pass a function that enqueues bytes to our ring. That gives us true interrupt/DMA-driven RX without touching IRQ or HAL callbacks.
- Or fully own the UART: disable/unuse libDaisy’s UART handling for that peripheral and implement our own `HAL_UART_Receive_IT` or DMA + callbacks. This means not using `UartHandler` on that UART to avoid symbol and handler clashes.
- Or keep what we have: the non-blocking poll-to-ring is simple, safe, and currently stable.

Given you don’t want extra complexity, I recommend we keep the current polling ring for now and only switch to `UartHandler::DmaListenStart` if we need more headroom at higher baud rates.

- If you want the `UartHandler` path, I can wire it so its listener callback feeds our ring, leaving parsing in the main loop.

+// UART DMA listen buffer and callback for libDaisy
+static uint8_t g_uart_dma_rxbuf[512];
+static void UartRxCallback(uint8_t* data,
+                           size_t   size,
+                           void*    context,
+                           daisy::UartHandler::Result result)
+{
+    (void)context;
+    if(result != daisy::UartHandler::Result::OK || data == nullptr || size == 0)
+        return;
+    for(size_t i = 0; i < size; ++i)
+    {
+        rx_enqueue_from_isr(data[i]);
+        g_uart_rx_count++;
+    }
+    g_last_uart_activity = System::GetNow();
+}
+
+// Initialize UART communication via libDaisy UartHandler with DMA listen
+void InitUART()
+{
+    hw.PrintLine("=== InitUART (UartHandler): FUNCTION ENTERED ===");
+
+    daisy::UartHandler::Config cfg{};
+    cfg.periph                  = daisy::UartHandler::Config::Peripheral::USART_1;
+    cfg.mode                    = daisy::UartHandler::Config::Mode::TX_RX;
+    cfg.stopbits                = daisy::UartHandler::Config::StopBits::BITS_1;
+    cfg.parity                  = daisy::UartHandler::Config::Parity::NONE;
+    cfg.wordlength              = daisy::UartHandler::Config::WordLength::BITS_8;
+    cfg.baudrate                = 115200;
+    cfg.pin_config.tx           = daisy::Pin(daisy::PORTB, 6); // PB6 = D13
+    cfg.pin_config.rx           = daisy::Pin(daisy::PORTB, 7); // PB7 = D14
+
+    if(g_uart.Init(cfg) != daisy::UartHandler::Result::OK)
+    {
+        hw.PrintLine("UartHandler init failed!");
+        return;
+    }
+    hw.PrintLine("UartHandler initialized: USART1 TX=PB6 RX=PB7 Baud=115200");
+
+    // Clear local buffers
+    memset(uart_rx_buffer, 0, sizeof(uart_rx_buffer));
+    memset(uart_tx_buffer, 0, sizeof(uart_tx_buffer));
+
+    // Prime TX buffer with a valid sync packet
+    uint8_t no_data_payload[] = {0};
+    size_t packet_len = ProtocolHandler::CreateGenericPacket(
+        uart_tx_buffer,
+        sizeof(uart_tx_buffer),
+        MSG_SYNC,
+        no_data_payload,
+        0);
+    if(packet_len > 0)
+        hw.PrintLine("TX buffer initialized with sync packet");
+
+    // Start circular DMA listen with callback
+    if(g_uart.DmaListenStart(g_uart_dma_rxbuf,
+                             sizeof(g_uart_dma_rxbuf),
+                             UartRxCallback,
+                             nullptr)
+       != daisy::UartHandler::Result::OK)
+    {
+        hw.PrintLine("UartHandler DMA listen start failed!");
+        return;
+    }
+    hw.PrintLine("UartHandler DMA listen started (circular) with callback");
+    hw.PrintLine("=== InitUART (UartHandler): FUNCTION COMPLETED SUCCESSFULLY ===");
+}
*** End Patch

---

Model: GPT-5, 175B, multimodal, rev 2025-08-15

- I switched Daisy to libDaisy `UartHandler` with DMA listen mode and a callback that enqueues bytes into our ring, keeping parsing in the main loop. I also added an ESP32 TX stress loop that bursts control changes and periodic preview requests for bidirectional load.

What changed (Daisy):
- Added `#include "per/uart.h"` and a global `daisy::UartHandler g_uart`.
- Replaced raw `HAL_UART_Transmit` with `g_uart.BlockingTransmit(...)` in `UART_Send`.
- Implemented `InitUART()` using `UartHandler::Config` for `USART_1` (PB6/PB7, 115200) and started `DmaListenStart` with a DMA RX buffer and `UartRxCallback`.
- `UartRxCallback` enqueues received bytes into our ring and updates counters.
- Left main-loop parser and proactive TX queue service intact.

What changed (ESP32):
- In `inter_mcu_tx_task`, added a high-rate stress generator:
  - Every tick: burst 8 `CONTROL_CHANGE` messages with varying values.
  - Every ~500 ms: send a `PREVIEW_REQ` to trigger Daisy→ESP32 `WAVE_CHUNK` traffic.
  - Existing RX task logs incoming frames and dispatches to listeners.

Build status:
- Daisy and ESP32 both build successfully.

Next:
- Flash both devices and monitor logs while running this stress. If you want, I can run the flash/monitor commands now and capture output.
